images.title: How American Farm Labor is Set to Evolve…
images.anchor: This essay examines the transformations in American farm labor since 1950, highlighting the forces that shaped its trajectory, the challenges that remain, and the potential impact of LLM-powered humanoid robots.

images.title: How American Farm Labor is Set to Evolve…
images.anchor: Within the next decade, humanoid robots powered by LLMs could take on specialized agricultural tasks, such as picking strawberries, pruning grapevines, or harvesting delicate fruits like cherries. These robots are designed to combine physical dexterity with real-time problem-solving capabilities, enabling them to handle tasks that have historically required skilled human labor.

images.title: How American Farm Labor is Set to Evolve…

Immigration reform remains critical, as the transition to full automation will take time, leaving an ongoing need for human labor. Programs like the H-2A visa must evolve to address immediate shortages while preparing for future changes.

images.title: Literacy in The United States and The World
Literacy in The United States and The World
images.anchor: II. The Current State of Literacy in the United States

images.title: Literacy in The United States and The World
images.anchor: The United States faces significant literacy challenges despite decades of educational reforms. According to PIAAC data, twenty-one percent of U.S. adults — representing approximately 43 million people — struggle with low English literacy skills. Within this group, 8.4 million adults score below Level 1, meaning they are functionally illiterate and unable to determine the meaning of sentences or locate single pieces of information in short texts. An additional 26.5 million adults score at Level 1, demonstrating only basic literacy skills, such as identifying vocabulary or deriving meaning from simple sentences. At the higher end, seventy-nine percent of adults score at Level 2 or above, which means they can perform basic comprehension tasks such as paraphrasing and making low-level inferences. These statistics highlight both the progress the U.S. has made and the gaps that persist.

images.title: Literacy in The United States and The World
images.anchor: III. The United States in a Global Context

images.title: Literacy in The United States and The World
images.anchor: Globally, literacy rates have improved substantially over the last two centuries, with developed nations achieving near-universal basic literacy by the 20th century. A historical overview of global literacy, as illustrated in the Our World in Data chart, shows that nations like the United States and the United Kingdom were early leaders in raising literacy rates, largely due to educational reforms and industrialization. In contrast, regions such as Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia still face substantial challenges, with adult literacy rates in some areas remaining below 60 percent.

images.title: Why Obergefell will not be Overturned
images.anchor: To overturn Obergefell, the Court would need to revisit not only substantive due process but also long-standing precedents affirming equality under the law — a much broader and more legally fraught endeavor.

images.title: Why Obergefell will not be Overturned
images.anchor: Since Obergefell, marriage equality has become a deeply ingrained part of American life. According to a 2023 Gallup poll, 71% of Americans support legal same-sex marriage, a dramatic increase from just two decades ago. Pew Research similarly found that 63% of Americans favor same-sex marriage, with only 34% opposed. This growing acceptance reflects a fundamental shift in societal attitudes, even among conservatives.
